title: Ice Secrets: How Scientists Learned About Earth's Past Climate
summary: Scientists study ancient ice in Antarctica to learn about Earth's climate from long ago. The ice has tiny air bubbles inside that hold old air. By looking at this ice, scientists can tell what the weather was like millions of years ago. They found that Earth was much warmer 3 million years ago. There were forests in places like Alaska and Greenland that are cold today. Sea levels were also higher. Over time, Earth cooled down. But a new study shows that the amount of greenhouse gases in the air did not drop much. This is surprising because greenhouse gases trap heat. Scientists are still trying to understand why Earth cooled even though greenhouse gases stayed almost the same. They also learned that the ocean cooled by about 2 degrees Celsius. This research helps us understand how climate changes over very long periods.

keywords.explanation: tiny pockets of old air trapped in ice
the usual weather in a place over a long time
gases that trap heat and warm the Earth
a big body of salt water

questions.question: What do scientists study to learn about Earth's past climate?
What is inside the ice that helps scientists?
How warm was Earth 3 million years ago compared to today?
What did scientists find surprising about greenhouse gases?
How much did the ocean cool over 3 million years?
Where do scientists find the ancient ice?

background_read: Earth's climate has changed many times. Ice ages are very cold periods when lots of ice covers the land. Between ice ages, it is warmer. Scientists use ice cores (long tubes of ice) to learn about past climates.
Article_Structure: WHO: Scientists from a group called COLDEX study ice in Antarctica.
WHAT: They found that Earth was warmer 3 million years ago and cooled, but greenhouse gases stayed almost the same.
WHERE: The ice comes from a place called Allan Hills in Antarctica.
WHY: They want to understand how climate changes over long time.

perspectives.description: They are excited to learn new things about past climate.
They can learn how science helps us understand Earth's history.
